sound_type: loud scolding chatter rattle -- aggressive, rapid, sustained machine-gun rattle of sharp notes, territorial fury, most vocal canopy mammal in PNW forests, E=5, conifer canopy habitat
ecosystem: conifer canopy. Dense coniferous forest -- Douglas-fir, western red cedar, Sitka spruce, western hemlock. The treetops. First true canopy mammal in the engine.
season: year-round. Does NOT hibernate. Active all year. Caches cones and fungi for winter use. One of few PNW mammals active through all seasons in the canopy.

species_pair.pair_type: Sciuridae SEXTET -- SIXTH consecutive Sciuridae (75-76-77-78-79-80). Matches Mustelidae sextet (63-68). SURPASSES Swallow quintet (26-30). TWO-WAY TIE at SIX for longest family run. Three marmots (Marmota, 75-77) + three squirrels (Urocitellus 78, Callospermophilus 79, Tamiasciurus 80). Six acoustic signatures. Six habitats. Alpine to canopy.

coordinates.description: Reference for conifer canopy habitat in the Cascade Range, Washington. Dense Douglas-fir and western red cedar forest. The Douglas Squirrel inhabits the canopy of PNW coniferous forests.

taxonomy_note.description: SIXTH CONSECUTIVE SCIURIDAE (75-76-77-78-79-80). Sciuridae sextet. Ties Mustelidae (63-68). Surpasses Swallows (26-30). NEW GENUS Tamiasciurus -- tree squirrels. Nineteenth mammalian entry. Seventeenth mammalian species. Seventh Rodentia. Sciuridae's sixth entry. Named for David Douglas (1799-1834).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Tamiasciurus from Greek tamias ('steward/hoarder') + skiouros ('squirrel,' from skia 'shadow' + oura 'tail'). douglasii honors David Douglas (1799-1834), Scottish botanist who explored the PNW. Described by Bachman (1839).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Tamiasciurus -- NEW GENUS in the engine. Fourth Sciuridae genus after Marmota (75-77), Urocitellus (78), Callospermophilus (79). Tree squirrels -- the first arboreal genus in the Sciuridae run.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- seventh Rodentia in the engine. Beaver (61-62), Yellow-bellied (75), Hoary (76), Olympic (77), Townsend's (78), Golden-mantled (79), Douglas (80).
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- nineteenth mammalian entry. Seventeenth mammalian species. Four orders. Nine families. Two infraclasses.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sciuridae's SIXTH entry at degree 80. Sixth consecutive (75-76-77-78-79-80). Sciuridae sextet ties Mustelidae sextet (63-68) for longest family run at SIX. Surpasses Swallow quintet (26-30).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Approximately 3,000-10,000 Hz. Broad frequency range reflecting the complex chatter structure.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. SCOLDING CHATTER RATTLE. Loud, aggressive, sustained territorial vocalization. Machine-gun rattle of sharp notes.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: 2-10+ seconds per chatter bout. Individual notes within the chatter: 0.02-0.05 seconds each. The LONGEST sustained vocalization in the Sciuridae run.
song_metrics.note_structure: Rapid sustained chatter -- many sharp notes in continuous stream. Not single notes (Golden-mantled) or short bursts (Townsend's) but CONTINUOUS RATTLING.
song_metrics.note_types: Scolding chatter rattle (primary, territorial), bark (short alarm), squeal (distress/aggression), purr (contentment/feeding).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Complex: sustained rattle with variation in tempo and intensity. The most complex vocal structure in the Sciuridae sextet.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp, aggressive, rattling. A continuous stream of percussive notes. Described as 'scolding' -- the squirrel sounds angry, always.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. LOUD. Audible at 200+ meters through dense conifer forest. The loudest and most persistent mammal vocalization in PNW forests. Dominates the forest soundscape.
song_metrics.tempo: Very fast. Individual notes at approximately 15-30 per second in rapid chatter. Sustained for seconds.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL DEFENSE. The chatter IS the territory. Directed at intruders (other squirrels, hikers, predators). Also alarm, but primarily territorial.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Chatter structure is consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: LOUD SCOLDING CHATTER RATTLE -- the 81st acoustic element. Aggressive sustained rattle. 3,000-10,000 Hz. E=5. Machine-gun quality. Most vocal mammal in PNW forests. First canopy acoustic element.

physical_description.overall: Small tree squirrel. 270-340 mm total length. 150-300 g. Dark olive-brown to reddish-brown above, orange-russet belly. Distinctive eye ring. Bushy tail. The PNW's signature tree squirrel.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 80: (1) DARK OLIVE-BROWN upperparts -- darker than other tree squirrels. (2) ORANGE-RUSSET BELLY -- distinctive warm underside. (3) PROMINENT EYE RING -- pale ring around eye. (4) BUSHY TAIL -- darker above, reddish below. (5) CONIFER CANOPY HABITAT -- always in trees. (6) LOUD PERSISTENT CHATTER -- the sound IS the identification.
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Dark olive-brown to reddish-brown above. Darker than most tree squirrels. BELLY: Orange-russet -- warm, distinctive. The warmest belly color in the Sciuridae sextet. EYE: Prominent pale eye ring. TAIL: Bushy, dark above, reddish-brown below with lighter fringe.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition. Granivorous. Specialized for conifer cone processing. The incisors strip cone scales to access seeds.
physical_description.field_id: In conifer forest: a small, dark, olive-brown squirrel with orange belly, prominent eye ring, running along branches or perched on a cone-scale midden. Identified most often by SOUND -- the loud, sustained, aggressive chatter that dominates the forest. If you hear a squirrel scolding you relentlessly in PNW conifer forest, it is a Douglas Squirrel.

nodes.title: Sciuridae Sextet (75-76-77-78-79-80) -- Ties Mustelidae Record at SIX
nodes.description: Six consecutive Sciuridae. Matches Mustelidae (63-68). Surpasses Swallow quintet (26-30). Three marmots (75-77) + three squirrels (78-80). Six acoustic signatures. Six habitats. TWO-WAY TIE at SIX.

nodes.title: Loud Scolding Chatter Rattle -- 81st Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Aggressive sustained rattle. 3,000-10,000 Hz. E=5. Machine-gun quality. 2-10+ seconds sustained. Most vocal mammal in PNW forests. First canopy acoustic element.

nodes.title: First True Canopy Mammal -- The Vertical Axis Opens
nodes.description: Conifer canopy habitat. Douglas-fir, western red cedar, Sitka spruce. The engine goes vertical for the first time. First arboreal mammal. Alpine to grassland to forest edge to canopy.

nodes.title: New Genus: Tamiasciurus
nodes.description: Fourth Sciuridae genus in the engine after Marmota (75-77), Urocitellus (78), Callospermophilus (79). Tree squirrels -- the first arboreal genus. 'Hoarder squirrel.'

nodes.title: John Muir's Favorite Animal
nodes.description: 'By far the most influential of all the Sierra animals.' Entire chapter in The Mountains of California (1894). The only mammal Muir gave a full chapter. The Chickaree.

nodes.title: Named for David Douglas (1799-1834)
nodes.description: David Douglas, Scottish botanist who explored the PNW and gave his name to the Douglas-fir. The squirrel that lives in the tree named for the same person. Described by Bachman (1839).

nodes.title: American Marten Canopy Predator-Prey
nodes.description: The American Marten (degree 65, Mustelidae sextet) is the primary predator. Both arboreal. Marten hunts Douglas Squirrel through the canopy. Cross-sextet predation: Mustelidae 65 preys on Sciuridae 80.

nodes.title: The Mates of State Parallel
nodes.description: Both E=5. Both fill all available space (chatter fills canopy / organ fills harmonic space). Both punch above their weight (small squirrel dominates forest soundscape / two-person duo sounds like full band). LOUD meets LOUD.

nodes.title: Conifer Seed Specialist Network
nodes.description: Shares conifer seed dependence with Pine Siskin (12) and Red Crossbill (34). Three species across two classes (Aves, Mammalia) all depending on conifer seeds. Convergent resource use.

nodes.title: Twentieth Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: TWO DECADES of original CSV -- no substitution needed for 20 consecutive species. CSV reliability milestone.

cross_references.relationship: SCIURIDAE SEXTET. Forest edge (79) to conifer canopy (80). Ground to treetops. Chip (E=4) vs. chatter (E=5). Two consecutive ground-to-canopy squirrels.
CANOPY PREDATOR-PREY! Marten (Mustelidae sextet, degree 65) is the primary predator of Douglas Squirrel (Sciuridae sextet, degree 80). Both arboreal. Cross-sextet predation through the canopy.
SCIURIDAE SEXTET (75-80). Three marmots + three squirrels. Six acoustic signatures. Six habitats: alpine rock, alpine meadow, Olympic alpine, grassland, forest edge, conifer canopy. Four genera.
CONIFER CANOPY SHARED. Both inhabit conifer treetops. Bird (Aves) and mammal (Mammalia) sharing canopy habitat. Cross-class canopy pair.
CONIFER SEED SPECIALIST. Both depend on conifer seeds. Crossbill extracts seeds with its specialized bill; Douglas Squirrel strips cone scales. Different extraction methods, same resource.
Nineteenth mammalian entry. Seventeenth species. Seventh Rodentia. Sciuridae sextet (75-80) ties Mustelidae (63-68). New genus Tamiasciurus. First canopy mammal.
Conifer canopy habitat. Key seed disperser (truffle spores through fecal pellets). Cone-scale middens as ecosystem feature. Year-round canopy activity. Forest sentinel.
